Early to mid-1980s, United Kingdom, United States and New Zealand

is a [subgenre](/wiki/Music_subgenre "Music subgenre") of [rock music](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music") that originated in the United Kingdom, United States and New Zealand in the early to mid-1980s. Although the term was originally used to describe rock music released through [independent record labels](/wiki/Independent_record_labels "Independent record labels"), by the 1990s it became more widely associated with the music such bands produced.

**Indie rock** is a [subgenre](/wiki/Music_subgenre "Music subgenre") of [rock music](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music") that originated in the United Kingdom, United States and New Zealand in the early to mid-1980s. Although the term was originally used to describe rock music released through [independent record labels](/wiki/Independent_record_labels "Independent record labels"), by the 1990s it became more widely associated with the music such bands produced.

The sound of indie rock has its origins in the [UK DIY music](/wiki/Early_UK_DIY "Early UK DIY") of [the Buzzcocks](/wiki/Buzzcocks "Buzzcocks"), [Desperate Bicycles](/wiki/Desperate_Bicycles "Desperate Bicycles")[[1]](#cite_note-:4-1) and [Television Personalities](/wiki/Television_Personalities "Television Personalities")[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) and the New Zealand [Dunedin sound](/wiki/Dunedin_sound "Dunedin sound") of [the Chills](/wiki/The_Chills "The Chills"), [Tall Dwarfs](/wiki/Tall_Dwarfs "Tall Dwarfs"),[[4]](#cite_note-4) [the Clean](/wiki/The_Clean "The Clean")[[5]](#cite_note-5) and [the Verlaines](/wiki/The_Verlaines "The Verlaines"), alongside [Australia](/wiki/Australian_indie_rock "Australian indie rock")'s [the Go-Betweens](/wiki/The_Go-Betweens "The Go-Betweens")[[6]](#cite_note-6) and early 1980s [college rock](/wiki/College_rock "College rock") radio stations who would frequently play [jangle pop](/wiki/Jangle_pop "Jangle pop") bands like [the Smiths](/wiki/The_Smiths "The Smiths") and [R.E.M.](/wiki/R.E.M. "R.E.M.") The genre solidified itself during the mid–1980s with *[NME](/wiki/NME "NME")*'s *[C86](/wiki/C86 "C86")* cassette in the United Kingdom and the underground success of [Sonic Youth](/wiki/Sonic_Youth "Sonic Youth"), [Dinosaur Jr.](/wiki/Dinosaur_Jr. "Dinosaur Jr.") and [Unrest](/wiki/Unrest_(American_band) "Unrest (American band)") in the United States. During the 1990s, indie rock bands like Sonic Youth, [the Pixies](/wiki/The_Pixies "The Pixies") and [Radiohead](/wiki/Radiohead "Radiohead") all released albums on major labels and subgenres like [slowcore](/wiki/Slowcore "Slowcore"), [Midwest emo](/wiki/Midwest_emo "Midwest emo"), [slacker rock](/wiki/Slacker_rock "Slacker rock") and [space rock](/wiki/Space_rock "Space rock") began. By this time, "indie" had evolved to refer to bands whose music was released on independent record labels, in addition to the record labels themselves. As the decade progressed many individual local scenes developed their own distinct takes on the genre: [baggy](/wiki/Baggy "Baggy") in [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester"); [grebo](/wiki/Grebo_(music) "Grebo (music)") in [Stourbridge](/wiki/Stourbridge "Stourbridge") and [Leicester](/wiki/Leicester "Leicester"); and [shoegaze](/wiki/Shoegaze "Shoegaze") in London and the [Thames Valley](/wiki/Thames_Valley "Thames Valley").

During the 1990s, the mainstream success of [grunge](/wiki/Grunge "Grunge") and [Britpop](/wiki/Britpop "Britpop"), two movements influenced by indie rock, brought increased attention to the genre and saw record labels use their independent status as a marketing tactic. This led to a split within indie rock: one side conforming to mainstream radio; the other becoming increasingly experimental. By this point, "indie rock" referred to the musical style rather than ties to the independent music scene. In the 2000s, indie rock reentered the mainstream through the [garage rock](/wiki/Garage_rock_revival "Garage rock revival") and [post-punk revival](/wiki/Post-punk_revival "Post-punk revival") and the influence of [the Strokes](/wiki/The_Strokes "The Strokes"), [the White Stripes](/wiki/The_White_Stripes "The White Stripes") and [the Libertines](/wiki/The_Libertines "The Libertines"). This success was exacerbated in the middle of the decade by [Bloc Party](/wiki/Bloc_Party "Bloc Party"), [Arctic Monkeys](/wiki/Arctic_Monkeys "Arctic Monkeys") and [the Killers](/wiki/The_Killers "The Killers"), while indie rock further proliferated into the 2000s [blog rock](/wiki/Blog_rock "Blog rock") era and the British [landfill indie](/wiki/Landfill_indie "Landfill indie") movement, as well as the [indie sleaze](/wiki/Indie_sleaze "Indie sleaze") aesthetic.

## Etymology and characteristics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_rock&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology and characteristics")]

> Lately we've been hearing the tag "'90s indie rock" used to describe bands ranging from [Waxahatchee](/wiki/Waxahatchee "Waxahatchee") to [Speedy Ortiz](/wiki/Speedy_Ortiz "Speedy Ortiz") to [Yuck](/wiki/Yuck_(band) "Yuck (band)"), and while our brain immediately turns a switch that associates the phrase with "sounds like [Pavement](/wiki/Pavement_(band) "Pavement (band)")", '90s indie rock was really as eclectic and undefinable as, well, contemporary indie rock.

Philip Cosores of *[Complex Networks](/wiki/Complex_Networks "Complex Networks")* (September 18, 2013) [[1]](https://www.complex.com/pigeons-and-planes/a/pigeons/best-90s-indie-rock-albums)

The terms "independent record label" or "independent music" were originally used in the 1930s to 1950s for American artists associated with [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"), [rhythm and blues](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues") and early [rock and roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll"), who were predominantly Black musicians initially sidelined by [major labels](/wiki/Major_labels "Major labels"), therefore relied on independent distribution to release their work.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

By the early 1980s, the earliest known use of the term "indie rock" was made in an article of *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* magazine, titled "Despite Hard Times, Indie Rock Labels Survive" on 15 January 1983 by writer Roman Kozak, in which he used a short-hand for the term "independent rock", to describe a growing trend of successful [independent record labels](/wiki/Independent_record_label "Independent record label") in New York that primarily focused on the emerging [alternative rock](/wiki/Alternative_rock "Alternative rock") music scene.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Indie rock originally described a style of alternative rock that was associated with small and relatively low-budget independent labels a [do-it-yourself](/wiki/DIY_ethic "DIY ethic") attitude. Drawing influences from [punk](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock"), [psychedelia](/wiki/Psychedelic_rock "Psychedelic rock") and [post-punk](/wiki/Post-punk "Post-punk").[[9]](#cite_note-BrownandVolgsten2006p.194-9) Although record deals were later often struck with [major corporate companies](/wiki/Major_label "Major label"), "indie rock" became more closely associated with a specific style of rock music rather than its mode of distribution.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-AllMusicIndie-11)

> Indie, in the beginning, was simply short for "independent" – the term was first applied to a bunch of British bands operating through independent record labels in the 1980s, most notably The Smiths. But indie-coded bands quickly became very, very popular. Defining indie as something commercially niche then made little sense, especially in the 2000s, when indie bands competed with rappers and pop stars for the top chart positions. So what are we left with? A songwriting sensibility that's a little weirder than down-the-line rock. Guitar work that often (but not always) tends towards the sharp and angular. A lot of blazers about too, but indie bands like to break rules.

Lucy Ford, Daisy Jones and Josiah Gogarty of *[GQ Magazine](/wiki/GQ_Magazine "GQ Magazine")* (February 12, 2025) [[2]](https://www.gq-magazine.co.uk/culture/article/best-indie-albums)

[AllMusic](/wiki/AllMusic "AllMusic") identifies indie rock as including a number of "varying musical approaches [not] compatible with mainstream tastes".[[12]](#cite_note-12) Linked by an ethos more than a musical approach, the indie rock movement encompassed a wide range of styles, from hard-edged, grunge-influenced bands, through do-it-yourself experimental bands like [Pavement](/wiki/Pavement_(band) "Pavement (band)"), to punk-folk singers such as [Ani DiFranco](/wiki/Ani_DiFranco "Ani DiFranco").[[13]](#cite_note-Bogdanov2002USAlternative-13) In his book *DIY Style: Fashion, Music and Global Digital Cultures*, Brent Luvaas described the genre as rooted in nostalgia, citing the influence of [garage rock](/wiki/Garage_rock "Garage rock") and [psychedelic rock](/wiki/Psychedelic_rock "Psychedelic rock") of the 1960s in progenitors [the Stone Roses](/wiki/The_Stone_Roses "The Stone Roses") and [the Smiths](/wiki/The_Smiths "The Smiths"), in addition to a lyrical preoccupation with literature.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

In this same vein, [Matthew Bannister](/wiki/Matthew_Bannister_(musician) "Matthew Bannister (musician)") defined indie rock as "small groups of white men playing guitars, influenced by punks and 1960s white pop/rock, within a broader discourse and practice of (degrees of) independence from mainstream musical values."[[15]](#cite_note-Huq_2016-15) According to anthropologist [Wendy Fonarow](/wiki/Wendy_Fonarow "Wendy Fonarow"), a key element of indie is the dichotomy between a "puritan ethos" and a "romantic one", with the former using austere ethics, and the latter being eccentric. This is best seen in the contrast between the indie music of United States and the United Kingdom in the 1990s, with British acts being flamboyant performers, while American acts used their lack of virtuosity as a mark of authenticity.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Indie rock is noted for having a relatively high proportion of female artists compared with preceding rock genres, a tendency exemplified by the development of the feminist-informed [riot grrrl](/wiki/Riot_grrrl "Riot grrrl") music of acts like [Bikini Kill](/wiki/Bikini_Kill "Bikini Kill"), [Bratmobile](/wiki/Bratmobile "Bratmobile"), [7 Year Bitch](/wiki/7_Year_Bitch "7 Year Bitch"), [Team Dresch](/wiki/Team_Dresch "Team Dresch") and [Huggy Bear](/wiki/Huggy_Bear_(band) "Huggy Bear (band)").[[17]](#cite_note-17) However, Cortney Harding pointed out that this sense of equality is not reflected in the number of women running indie labels.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

## Influences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_rock&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Influences")]

### 1960s–1970s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_rock&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1960s–1970s")]

See also: [Proto-punk](/wiki/Proto-punk "Proto-punk"), [Glam rock](/wiki/Glam_rock "Glam rock"), [Power pop](/wiki/Power_pop "Power pop"), and [Art rock](/wiki/Art_rock "Art rock")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Velvet_Underground_%26_Nico_publicity_photo_%28retouched%29.jpg/250px-Velvet_Underground_%26_Nico_publicity_photo_%28retouched%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Velvet_Underground_%26_Nico_publicity_photo_(retouched).jpg)

[The Velvet Underground](/wiki/The_Velvet_Underground "The Velvet Underground") was an influential [experimental rock](/wiki/Experimental_rock "Experimental rock") act during the late 1960s.

[The Velvet Underground](/wiki/The_Velvet_Underground "The Velvet Underground") and [the Kinks](/wiki/The_Kinks "The Kinks") have been cited as early musical influences on indie rock, with [David Lowery](/wiki/David_Lowery_(musician) "David Lowery (musician)") of the band [Cracker](/wiki/Cracker_(band) "Cracker (band)") stating: "The Kinks were like the first indie rock band, in a weird way".[[19]](#cite_note-19) Furthermore, [*Pitchfork*](/wiki/Pitchfork_(website) "Pitchfork (website)") cited [the Beach Boys](/wiki/The_Beach_Boys "The Beach Boys"),[[20]](#cite_note-20) as well as [the Byrds](/wiki/The_Byrds "The Byrds") as pivotal influences, noting that "without the Byrds, the entirety of [college rock](/wiki/College_rock "College rock")—and, from it, indie rock—wouldn't exist".[[21]](#cite_note-21) Other influences include [Syd Barrett](/wiki/Syd_Barrett "Syd Barrett") of [Pink Floyd](/wiki/Pink_Floyd "Pink Floyd") and [Alex Chilton](/wiki/Alex_Chilton "Alex Chilton")'s[[22]](#cite_note-22) [Big Star](/wiki/Big_Star "Big Star"), with [the Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian") noting that Barrett's "influence became built into the music we now know as indie rock",[[23]](#cite_note-23) and describing [Big Star](/wiki/Big_Star "Big Star") as the "ur-band" for "emotionally acute indie-rock".[[24]](#cite_note-24) Alongside, ex-[Roxy Music](/wiki/Roxy_Music "Roxy Music") member, [Brian Eno](/wiki/Brian_Eno "Brian Eno")'s [debut](/wiki/Here_Come_the_Warm_Jets "Here Come the Warm Jets") and [sophomore](/wiki/Taking_Tiger_Mountain_(By_Strategy) "Taking Tiger Mountain (By Strategy)") albums.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

In New York's [CBGB](/wiki/CBGB "CBGB") scene, [Patti Smith](/wiki/Patti_Smith "Patti Smith")[[26]](#cite_note-26) became the first punk artist to self-release a single with her 1974 cover of "[Hey Joe](/wiki/Hey_Joe "Hey Joe")", featuring "[Piss Factory](/wiki/Piss_Factory "Piss Factory")" as the B-side. This was later followed by [Television](/wiki/Television_(band) "Television (band)")'s "[Little Johnny Jewel](/wiki/Little_Johnny_Jewel "Little Johnny Jewel")" in October 1975, their melodic guitar interplay on [Marquee Moon](/wiki/Marquee_Moon "Marquee Moon") proved influential to the early indie scene.[[27]](#cite_note-27) [Pere Ubu](/wiki/Pere_Ubu "Pere Ubu") also self-released their [debut single](/wiki/30_Seconds_Over_Tokyo_(song) "30 Seconds Over Tokyo (song)") in December 1975.[[28]](#cite_note-28) Other influences included [proto-punk](/wiki/Proto-punk "Proto-punk") artists [the Stooges](/wiki/The_Stooges "The Stooges")[[29]](#cite_note-29) and [the Modern Lovers](/wiki/The_Modern_Lovers "The Modern Lovers").[[30]](#cite_note-30)

### Late 1970s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_rock&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Late 1970s")]

See also: [Sex Pistols at the Lesser Free Trade Hall](/wiki/Sex_Pistols_at_the_Lesser_Free_Trade_Hall "Sex Pistols at the Lesser Free Trade Hall") and [Post-punk](/wiki/Post-punk "Post-punk")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Sex_Pistols_June_4_1976.jpg/250px-Sex_Pistols_June_4_1976.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sex_Pistols_June_4_1976.jpg)

[Sex Pistols](/wiki/Sex_Pistols "Sex Pistols") at Manchester's [Lesser Free Trade Hall](/wiki/Lesser_Free_Trade_Hall "Lesser Free Trade Hall") on 4 June and 20 July 1976 inspired the formation of [Joy Division](/wiki/Joy_Division "Joy Division"), [the Fall](/wiki/The_Fall_(band) "The Fall (band)"), [the Smiths](/wiki/The_Smiths "The Smiths"), [Simply Red](/wiki/Simply_Red "Simply Red"), [Magazine](/wiki/Magazine_(band) "Magazine (band)"), [Buzzcocks](/wiki/Buzzcocks "Buzzcocks"), [Factory](/wiki/Factory_Records "Factory Records") and [Creation Records](/wiki/Creation_Records "Creation Records")

During the advent of [punk rock](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock") in the late 1970s, bands began to embrace and popularize a [DIY ethic](/wiki/DIY_ethic "DIY ethic") that encouraged anyone, regardless of skill or musical background,[[31]](#cite_note-King2012-31) to form a band. On June 4 and July 20, 1976, [the Sex Pistols](/wiki/The_Sex_Pistols "The Sex Pistols"), performed at the [Lesser Free Trade Hall](/wiki/Lesser_Free_Trade_Hall "Lesser Free Trade Hall") in Manchester, which inspired the formation of influential groups like [the Smiths](/wiki/The_Smiths "The Smiths"), [Buzzcocks](/wiki/Buzzcocks "Buzzcocks"), [the Fall](/wiki/The_Fall_(band) "The Fall (band)") and [Joy Division](/wiki/Joy_Division "Joy Division") as well as the launch of influential independent record labels such as [Factory](/wiki/Factory_Records "Factory Records") and [Creation Records](/wiki/Creation_Records "Creation Records"), as [Tony Wilson](/wiki/Tony_Wilson "Tony Wilson") and [Alan McGee](/wiki/Alan_McGee "Alan McGee") cited the performance as inspiring their interest in British alternative music.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

Furthermore, independent record labels became integral to the early years of punk rock musical distribution, as seen with [Beserkley Records](/wiki/Beserkley_Records "Beserkley Records") in the US, who put out the [debut album](/wiki/The_Modern_Lovers_(album) "The Modern Lovers (album)") of [The Modern Lovers](/wiki/The_Modern_Lovers_(album) "The Modern Lovers (album)") which was recorded years earlier. In the UK, indie label [Stiff Records](/wiki/Stiff_Records "Stiff Records") released the first UK punk single "[New Rose](/wiki/New_Rose "New Rose")" by [the Damned](/wiki/The_Damned_(band) "The Damned (band)"). In [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), Brisbane band [the Saints](/wiki/The_Saints_(Australian_band) "The Saints (Australian band)") had their first punk release outside the US, "[(I'm) Stranded](/wiki/(I%27m)_Stranded_(song) "(I'm) Stranded (song)")", on their own "Fatal Records" label. This was followed by [the Go-Betweens](/wiki/The_Go-Betweens "The Go-Betweens") releasing "[Lee Remick](/wiki/Lee_Remick_(The_Go-Betweens_song) "Lee Remick (The Go-Betweens song)")" a few months later.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

By the late 1970s, the [post-punk](/wiki/Post-punk "Post-punk") scene emerged in the UK, encapsulating bands that while retaining the DIY ethos of punk, began to push its boundaries. Artists such as [the Cure](/wiki/The_Cure "The Cure"), [the Monochrome Set](/wiki/The_Monochrome_Set "The Monochrome Set"),[[34]](#cite_note-34) [Felt](/wiki/Felt_(band) "Felt (band)"), [the Raincoats](/wiki/The_Raincoats "The Raincoats") and [Young Marble Giants](/wiki/Young_Marble_Giants "Young Marble Giants") who were mostly signed to independent labels like [Rough Trade](/wiki/Rough_Trade_Records "Rough Trade Records"), [Factory Records](/wiki/Factory_Records "Factory Records") and [Cherry Red](/wiki/Cherry_Red_Records "Cherry Red Records"), served as a foundational influence on indie rock and later alternative music. Additionally, art punk band [Wire](/wiki/Wire_(band) "Wire (band)") influenced later acts such as [Guided by Voices](/wiki/Guided_by_Voices "Guided by Voices") and [Hüsker Dü](/wiki/H%C3%BCsker_D%C3%BC "Hüsker Dü").[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-:3-36) Likewise, [the Soft Boys](/wiki/The_Soft_Boys "The Soft Boys"), became cult favorites in the US where bands like [R.E.M.](/wiki/R.E.M. "R.E.M.") and [the Replacements](/wiki/The_Replacements_(band) "The Replacements (band)") "memorized every lick".[[37]](#cite_note-37)

Scottish [post-punk](/wiki/Post-punk "Post-punk") bands, [Orange Juice](/wiki/Orange_Juice_(band) "Orange Juice (band)"), [Josef K](/wiki/Josef_K_(band) "Josef K (band)") and later [the Vaselines](/wiki/The_Vaselines "The Vaselines") also proved to be influential.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

American groups like [the Feelies](/wiki/The_Feelies "The Feelies"),[[39]](#cite_note-39) [Mission of Burma](/wiki/Mission_of_Burma "Mission of Burma"),[[40]](#cite_note-40) [Talking Heads](/wiki/Talking_Heads "Talking Heads")[[41]](#cite_note-41) and [the Embarrassment](/wiki/The_Embarrassment "The Embarrassment")[[42]](#cite_note-42)—the latter an influence on [R.E.M.](/wiki/R.E.M. "R.E.M.")[[43]](#cite_note-43) were also important to the development of indie rock.

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_rock&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Background")]

See also: [Independent music](/wiki/Independent_music "Independent music"), [Independent record label](/wiki/Independent_record_label "Independent record label"), and [UK Indie Chart](/wiki/UK_Indie_Chart "UK Indie Chart")

Before [punk rock](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock") and the [independent music](/wiki/Independent_music "Independent music") boom of the late 1970s to early 1980s, independent music already had a rich history of promoting developments in popular music, particularly in genres overlooked by major labels. Prior to the late 1970s, major record companies held so much power that independent labels struggled to establish themselves, as they often failed commercially or were eventually absorbed by major labels. However, this dynamic began to change in 1979 when [Rough Trade](/wiki/Rough_Trade_Records "Rough Trade Records") released the album *[Inflammable Material](/wiki/Inflammable_Material "Inflammable Material")* by [Stiff Little Fingers](/wiki/Stiff_Little_Fingers "Stiff Little Fingers") which went on to be the first independently-released album to sell over 100,000 copies and enter the [UK Top 20](/wiki/UK_Top_20 "UK Top 20"). This success sparked major record companies' interest in independent music and by the end of the decade, the establishment of the [UK indie charts](/wiki/UK_Independent_Singles_and_Albums_Charts "UK Independent Singles and Albums Charts") signaled the growing popularity of the movement.[[44]](#cite_note-:2-44) The [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") documentary "*Do it Yourself: The Story of Rough Trade*"[[45]](#cite_note-45) stated that:

> "[...] when Rough Trade began in 1976 there were about a dozen independent labels in Britain, by the end of the decade there were over 800."

Other notable early indie labels include [Mute](/wiki/Mute_Records "Mute Records"), [4AD](/wiki/4AD "4AD"), [Factory](/wiki/Factory_Records "Factory Records"), [Beggars Banquet](/wiki/Beggars_Banquet_Records "Beggars Banquet Records") and [Creation Records](/wiki/Creation_Records "Creation Records").[[31]](#cite_note-King2012-31)

### Early UK DIY movement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_rock&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Early UK DIY movement")]

See also: [DIY punk ethic](/wiki/DIY_punk_ethic "DIY punk ethic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Television_Personalities_%285363667797%29.jpg/250px-Television_Personalities_%285363667797%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Television_Personalities_(5363667797).jpg)

Dan Treacy's [Television Personalities](/wiki/Television_Personalities "Television Personalities"), one of the leading bands of the UK DIY wave and forerunners to [indie pop](/wiki/Indie_pop "Indie pop")

The *[BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC")* documentary *Music for Misfits: The Story of Indie* remarks that the self-publication of the *[Spiral Scratch](/wiki/Spiral_Scratch_(EP) "Spiral Scratch (EP)")* EP by Manchester [punk rock](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock") band the [Buzzcocks](/wiki/Buzzcocks "Buzzcocks"), on their independent record label [New Hormones](/wiki/New_Hormones "New Hormones") on 29 January 1977, was a pivotal moment in the development of indie rock, as well as the wider English independent music scene.[[46]](#cite_note-A_definition_of_indie_music-46)[[47]](#cite_note-bbc.co.uk-47) Writer Kevin Dunn's *Global Punk* highlights how the [Buzzcocks](/wiki/Buzzcocks "Buzzcocks")' debut EP inspired a wave of independent DIY releases across the United Kingdom: "...the EP literally showed how one could make a record, with the details of the recording process (e.g., number of takes and over-dubs) and pressing costs printed right on the record cover."[[48]](#cite_note-48)

Similarly, the earliest releases by the [Desperate Bicycles](/wiki/The_Desperate_Bicycles "The Desperate Bicycles")[[1]](#cite_note-:4-1) showed listeners how to produce and distribute their own records independently at very low cost, with the sole objective of undermining the monopoly that major record labels held over the wider music industry. Both the Buzzcocks and Desperate Bicycles helped inspire a wave of [DIY punk](/wiki/DIY_punk_ethic "DIY punk ethic") bands like ['O' Level](/wiki/%27O%27_Level "'O' Level"), [Television Personalities](/wiki/Television_Personalities "Television Personalities"), [Swell Maps](/wiki/Swell_Maps "Swell Maps"), and others—who followed suit in producing and distributing their own records.[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50) Distribution was further improved with the establishment of 'The Cartel', an association of companies like [Red Rhino](/wiki/Red_Rhino "Red Rhino") and [Rough Trade Records](/wiki/Rough_Trade_Records "Rough Trade Records") who would take the releases from these small labels and get them into [record shops](/wiki/Record_stores "Record stores") nationwide.[[51]](#cite_note-Lazell-51) [Stephen Malkmus](/wiki/Stephen_Malkmus "Stephen Malkmus") later cited the British DIY era as a foundational influence on [Pavement](/wiki/Pavement_(band) "Pavement (band)").[[52]](#cite_note-52)

### Dunedin sound

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_rock&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Dunedin sound")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/The_Chills%2C_Indietracks_2014_10.jpg/250px-The_Chills%2C_Indietracks_2014_10.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Chills,_Indietracks_2014_10.jpg)

[The Chills](/wiki/The_Chills "The Chills") performing at [Indietracks](/wiki/Indietracks "Indietracks") in rural [Derbyshire](/wiki/Derbyshire "Derbyshire") in 2014

The city of [Dunedin](/wiki/Dunedin "Dunedin"), New Zealand, produced many of the bands that formed the cornerstone of the independent record label [Flying Nun Records](/wiki/Flying_Nun_Records "Flying Nun Records"). These artists defined the [Dunedin sound](/wiki/Dunedin_sound "Dunedin sound"), which would be particularly influential on the development of indie rock's sound.[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-McConnell-54)[[55]](#cite_note-daily.bandcamp.com-55) According to [Audioculture](/wiki/AudioCulture "AudioCulture"), one of the earliest Dunedin Sound band was [Chris Knox](/wiki/Chris_Knox "Chris Knox")'s band [the Enemy](/wiki/The_Enemy_(New_Zealand_band) "The Enemy (New Zealand band)"), which emerged as a post-punk group, whose members also included [Alec Bathgate](/wiki/Alec_Bathgate "Alec Bathgate"). Although the group was only around for a short time, their shows impressed the teenage musicians that came to see them, including a young [Shayne Carter](/wiki/Shayne_Carter "Shayne Carter"), who went on to form [Bored Games](/wiki/Bored_Games "Bored Games"), [the DoubleHappys](/wiki/The_DoubleHappys "The DoubleHappys") and [Straitjacket Fits](/wiki/Straitjacket_Fits "Straitjacket Fits"). Knox later formed another short-lived punk band called [Toy Love](/wiki/Toy_Love "Toy Love"), and after it broke up, he went on to start the influential band [Tall Dwarfs](/wiki/Tall_Dwarfs "Tall Dwarfs"), who were an integral influence on the emergence of home-recorded [lo-fi indie](/wiki/Lo-fi_music "Lo-fi music").[[56]](#cite_note-Allmusic-56) The punk-inspired aspects of the scene were often inspired by opposition to [Robert Muldoon](/wiki/Robert_Muldoon "Robert Muldoon") and his government, which prompted satire or outright criticism.[[57]](#cite_note-57) The scene saw bands take influence from punk rock, but strip away its aggression for a [reverb](/wiki/Reverb "Reverb") heavy, [pop](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music")–influenced sound. Marked by [the Clean](/wiki/The_Clean "The Clean")'s 1981 debut single "Tally-Ho!" and 1982's *[Dunedin Double](/wiki/Dunedin_Double_(EP) "Dunedin Double (EP)")* EP featuring [the Chills](/wiki/The_Chills "The Chills"), [Sneaky Feelings](/wiki/Sneaky_Feelings "Sneaky Feelings"), [the Verlaines](/wiki/The_Verlaines "The Verlaines") and [the Stones](/wiki/The_Stones_(band) "The Stones (band)"), its guitars were often jangly and droning and vocals indistinct.[[58]](#cite_note-58) The following years, the Dunedin sound spread to other New Zealand cities such as [Christchurch](/wiki/Christchurch "Christchurch") or [Auckland](/wiki/Auckland "Auckland").[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[55]](#cite_note-daily.bandcamp.com-55)[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[54]](#cite_note-McConnell-54)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_rock&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: History")]

### Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_rock&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Origins")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Jesus_and_Mary_Chain_2007.jpg/250px-Jesus_and_Mary_Chain_2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jesus_and_Mary_Chain_2007.jpg)

British band [the Jesus and Mary Chain](/wiki/The_Jesus_and_Mary_Chain "The Jesus and Mary Chain") performing in California in 2007

See also: [College rock](/wiki/College_rock "College rock") and [Jangle pop](/wiki/Jangle_pop "Jangle pop")

The early to mid–1980s saw the growing popularity of [college radio](/wiki/Campus_radio "Campus radio") stations, primarily in the United States, who would play independent artists of various genres, including [alternative rock](/wiki/Alternative_rock "Alternative rock"), [new wave](/wiki/New_wave_music "New wave music"), [post-hardcore](/wiki/Post-hardcore "Post-hardcore") and [post-punk](/wiki/Post-punk "Post-punk"). The bands broadcast on these stations became dubbed "[college rock](/wiki/College_rock "College rock")" by fans, another term which lacked any stylistic implication. The most prominent college rock bands were [jangle pop](/wiki/Jangle_pop "Jangle pop") groups [R.E.M.](/wiki/R.E.M. "R.E.M."), from the US, and [the Smiths](/wiki/The_Smiths "The Smiths"), from the UK, who [Matthew Bannister](/wiki/Matthew_Bannister_(musician) "Matthew Bannister (musician)") states were the earliest indie rock groups.[[61]](#cite_note-Bannister-61) These bands' influence was quickly seen in the formation of [Let's Active](/wiki/Let%27s_Active "Let's Active"), [the Housemartins](/wiki/The_Housemartins "The Housemartins") and [the La's](/wiki/The_La%27s "The La's").[[62]](#cite_note-AMCollegeRock-62) By this time, the term "indie rock" had begun to be used to describe the bands who produced music on independent record labels, rather than simply the record labels themselves. This made it the only genre at the time which was defined by the methods by which the music was distributed rather than the sound of said music.[[9]](#cite_note-BrownandVolgsten2006p.194-9)

Journalist Steve Taylor also cited the bands involved in the [Paisley Underground](/wiki/Paisley_Underground "Paisley Underground") scene as early indie groups.[[63]](#cite_note-63) In the following years [the Jesus and Mary Chain](/wiki/The_Jesus_and_Mary_Chain "The Jesus and Mary Chain") and Flying Nun Records bands like the [Jean-Paul Sartre Experience](/wiki/Jean-Paul_Sartre_Experience "Jean-Paul Sartre Experience") morphed the genre into a slower, darker and more hypnotic style.[[61]](#cite_note-Bannister-61) The number of college radio stations in the US decreased significantly following [NPR](/wiki/NPR "NPR")'s lobbying against noncommercial station during the 1980s. In turn, the name "college rock" fell into disfavour, soon being replaced by "indie".[[64]](#cite_note-64)

### Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_rock&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Development")]

See also: [C86](/wiki/C86 "C86")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Bilinda_Butcher_%28cropped_2%29.jpg/250px-Bilinda_Butcher_%28cropped_2%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bilinda_Butcher_(cropped_2).jpg)

[My Bloody Valentine](/wiki/My_Bloody_Valentine_(band) "My Bloody Valentine (band)") pioneered the indie rock subgenre [shoegaze](/wiki/Shoegaze "Shoegaze").

In the United Kingdom, *[NME](/wiki/NME "NME")* released the *[C86](/wiki/C86 "C86")* compilation cassette, which consisted of tracks by groups including [Primal Scream](/wiki/Primal_Scream "Primal Scream"), [the Pastels](/wiki/The_Pastels "The Pastels") and [the Wedding Present](/wiki/The_Wedding_Present "The Wedding Present"). Intended to showcase the UK's current independent music scene, the album was made up of groups combining elements of jangle pop, post-punk and [Phil Spector](/wiki/Phil_Spector "Phil Spector") indebted [Walls of Sound](/wiki/Wall_of_Sound "Wall of Sound"). In 2006, [Bob Stanley](/wiki/Bob_Stanley_(musician) "Bob Stanley (musician)") called it "the beginning of indie music".[[65]](#cite_note-65) C86 became a descriptor in its own right, describing not only the bands on the tape but also bands who it influenced, often used alongside terms like "anorak pop" and "shambling".[[66]](#cite_note-66) Some C86 bands found significant commercial success: [the Soup Dragons](/wiki/The_Soup_Dragons "The Soup Dragons") went on to sell out [Madison Square Garden](/wiki/Madison_Square_Garden "Madison Square Garden"); Primal Scream were critically acclaimed, receiving the first ever [Mercury Prize](/wiki/Mercury_Prize "Mercury Prize") in 1992; the Wedding Present charted eighteen times in the Top 40; however many bands in its twenty-two track runtime also fell into obscurity.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

In the United States, the popularity of R.E.M. allowed those disliking of [hardcore punk](/wiki/Hardcore_punk "Hardcore punk")'s aggression and militancy to become a part of the [underground music](/wiki/Underground_music "Underground music") scene. This empowered an array of musicians, particularly those in what would become the [post-hardcore](/wiki/Post-hardcore "Post-hardcore") scene as led by [the Minutemen](/wiki/Minutemen_(band) "Minutemen (band)")[[68]](#cite_note-AzP94-68) who in the hardcore scene were often met with hostility, with [Pitchfork](/wiki/Pitchfork_(website) "Pitchfork (website)")[[69]](#cite_note-69) citing "the first time they opened for [Black Flag](/wiki/Black_Flag_(band) "Black Flag (band)"), the Minutemen were showered with spit from the crowd", further stating that "they just weren't that hardcore". Despite this, they've been credited with providing "the life blood of indie rock for decades".[[68]](#cite_note-AzP94-68)[[70]](#cite_note-70)[[71]](#cite_note-71) Similarly, [Wipers](/wiki/Wipers_(band) "Wipers (band)"), who with their melodic riffs and emphasis on guitar leads and solos, also stood out in the hardcore scene, influencing emerging indie artists such as [J Mascis](/wiki/J_Mascis "J Mascis") of [Dinosaur Jr](/wiki/Dinosaur_Jr. "Dinosaur Jr."), helping re-introduce guitar virtuosity back into alternative music.[[72]](#cite_note-72)[[73]](#cite_note-73)

Furthermore, major labels began to pursue underground bands, with both [Hüsker Dü](/wiki/H%C3%BCsker_D%C3%BC "Hüsker Dü") and [the Replacements](/wiki/The_Replacements_(band) "The Replacements (band)") releasing albums on majors in the middle of the decade. While these albums did not see the same success as R.E.M., and major labels soon lost interest in the scene, they did have a large impact on younger bands. Some groups like [Camper Van Beethoven](/wiki/Camper_Van_Beethoven "Camper Van Beethoven"),[[74]](#cite_note-74) [the Dead Milkmen](/wiki/The_Dead_Milkmen "The Dead Milkmen")[[75]](#cite_note-75) and [Violent Femmes](/wiki/Violent_Femmes "Violent Femmes")[[76]](#cite_note-76) infused the scene with sardonic humor and irony. In the following years, [Sonic Youth](/wiki/Sonic_Youth "Sonic Youth"), [Dinosaur Jr.](/wiki/Dinosaur_Jr. "Dinosaur Jr.") and [Unrest](/wiki/Unrest_(American_band) "Unrest (American band)") began to release music on independent labels indebted to these bands, and soon too picked up the categorisation of indie rock.[[77]](#cite_note-77) As the 1980s closed, both Sonic Youth and [the Pixies](/wiki/The_Pixies "The Pixies") signed to major labels.[[78]](#cite_note-78)

In the late 1980s, the indie rock subgenre [shoegaze](/wiki/Shoegaze "Shoegaze") emerged, as a continuation of the wall of sound production being used by groups like the Jesus and Mary Chain. The genre merged this with influences from [Dinosaur Jr.](/wiki/Dinosaur_Jr. "Dinosaur Jr.") and the [Cocteau Twins](/wiki/Cocteau_Twins "Cocteau Twins"), to create a dark and droning style so cacophonous that instruments were often indistinguishable. The genre was pioneered by [My Bloody Valentine](/wiki/My_Bloody_Valentine_(band) "My Bloody Valentine (band)") on their early EPs and debut album *[Isn't Anything](/wiki/Isn%27t_Anything "Isn't Anything")*.[[79]](#cite_note-AMShoegaze-79) The band's style influenced a wave of bands in London and the [Thames Valley](/wiki/Thames_Valley "Thames Valley") area including [Chapterhouse](/wiki/Chapterhouse_(band) "Chapterhouse (band)"), [Moose](/wiki/Moose_(band) "Moose (band)") and [Lush](/wiki/Lush_(band) "Lush (band)"). This scene was collectively termed "the Scene That Celebrates Itself" by *[Melody Maker](/wiki/Melody_Maker "Melody Maker")'s* [Steve Sutherland](/wiki/Steve_Sutherland_(DJ) "Steve Sutherland (DJ)") in 1990.[[80]](#cite_note-Larkin-80)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Stone_Roses-17-07-2012_Milan.JPG/250px-Stone_Roses-17-07-2012_Milan.JPG)](/wiki/File:Stone_Roses-17-07-2012_Milan.JPG)

[The Stone Roses](/wiki/The_Stone_Roses "The Stone Roses")' 1990 [Spike Island](/wiki/Spike_Island_(concert) "Spike Island (concert)") concert was the highest attendance performance by an independent artist of its time.

[Madchester](/wiki/Madchester "Madchester") was another style and scene that originated in the late 1980s. Defined by its merger of C86 indie rock, [dance music](/wiki/Dance_music "Dance music") and [Hedonist](/wiki/Hedonist "Hedonist") rave culture, particularly its emphasis on the use of psychedelic drugs, the scene was centred in [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester").[[81]](#cite_note-Barnett_2020-81)[[82]](#cite_note-82) The scene was based around [the Haçienda](/wiki/The_Ha%C3%A7ienda "The Haçienda") nightclub, which opened in May 1982 as an initiative of [Factory Records](/wiki/Factory_Records "Factory Records"). For the first few years of its life, the club played predominantly club-oriented pop music and hosted performances by artists including [New Order](/wiki/New_Order_(band) "New Order (band)"), [Cabaret Voltaire](/wiki/Cabaret_Voltaire_(band) "Cabaret Voltaire (band)"), [Culture Club](/wiki/Culture_Club "Culture Club"), [Thompson Twins](/wiki/Thompson_Twins "Thompson Twins") and the Smiths.[[83]](#cite_note-83) The Madchester movement burgeoned by 1989, with the success of [the Happy Mondays](/wiki/The_Happy_Mondays "The Happy Mondays") second album *[Bummed](/wiki/Bummed "Bummed")* and [the Stone Roses](/wiki/The_Stone_Roses "The Stone Roses")' [self-titled debut](/wiki/The_Stone_Roses_(album) "The Stone Roses (album)"), which became the most influential work in the scene. In the following years, addition high profile acts included [the Charlatans](/wiki/The_Charlatans_(English_band) "The Charlatans (English band)"), [808 State](/wiki/808_State "808 State") and the [Inspiral Carpets](/wiki/Inspiral_Carpets "Inspiral Carpets").[[84]](#cite_note-84)

The Madchester scene's distinct combination of indie rock and dance music became termed [indie dance](/wiki/Indie_dance "Indie dance") by critics, or more specifically the subgenre [baggy](/wiki/Baggy "Baggy").[[85]](#cite_note-85) Madchester and baggy's most infamous moment was the 27 May 1990 [Spike Island](/wiki/Spike_Island_(concert) "Spike Island (concert)") concert headlined by the Stone Roses. With an attendance of around 28,000 and lasting twelve hours, it was the first event of its size and kind to be hosted by an independent act.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

In [Stourbridge](/wiki/Stourbridge "Stourbridge"), a scene of indie bands who took influence from [electronic](/wiki/Electronic_music "Electronic music"), punk, [folk](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") and [hip-hop music](/wiki/Hip-hop_music "Hip-hop music") emerged, dubbed [grebo](/wiki/Grebo_(music) "Grebo (music)") by critics. Fronted by [Pop Will Eat Itself](/wiki/Pop_Will_Eat_Itself "Pop Will Eat Itself"), [the Wonder Stuff](/wiki/The_Wonder_Stuff "The Wonder Stuff") and [Ned's Atomic Dustbin](/wiki/Ned%27s_Atomic_Dustbin "Ned's Atomic Dustbin"), "grebo" was broadly defined, and was used more as a name for the Stourbridge scene than as a genre label. However, the bands quickly gained attention: Pop Will Eat Itself's 1989 singles "[Wise Up! Sucker](/wiki/Wise_Up!_Sucker "Wise Up! Sucker")" and "[Can U Dig It?](/wiki/Can_U_Dig_It%3F "Can U Dig It?")" both entered the UK Top 40 and Stourbridge briefly became a tourist attraction for young indie rock fans. The seminal albums from the scene were released between 1989 and 1993: the Wonder Stuff's *[Hup](/wiki/Hup_(album) "Hup (album)")* and *[Never Loved Elvis](/wiki/Never_Loved_Elvis "Never Loved Elvis")*; Ned's Atomic Dustbin's *[God Fodder](/wiki/God_Fodder "God Fodder")* and *[Are You Normal?](/wiki/Are_You_Normal%3F "Are You Normal?")*; and Pop Will Eat Itself's *[This Is the Day...This Is the Hour...This Is This!](/wiki/This_Is_the_Day...This_Is_the_Hour...This_Is_This! "This Is the Day...This Is the Hour...This Is This!")* and *[The Looks or the Lifestyle?](/wiki/The_Looks_or_the_Lifestyle%3F "The Looks or the Lifestyle?")*. In this period, the scene's bands became fixtures, sometimes headliners, at [Reading Festival](/wiki/Reading_Festival "Reading Festival"), sold millions of albums and were frequently featured on the covers of magazines like *NME* and *[Melody Maker](/wiki/Melody_Maker "Melody Maker")*.[[87]](#cite_note-Guardian_Grebo-87) Grebo bands were distinct from prior indie rock groups not only because of their broad influences, but their subversion of the twee or unhappy moods of most other bands in the genre, and their pursuit of a heavier sound and aesthetic. The scene came to include the stylistically similar bands of nearby Leicester: [the Bomb Party](/wiki/The_Bomb_Party "The Bomb Party"), [Gaye Bykers on Acid](/wiki/Gaye_Bykers_on_Acid "Gaye Bykers on Acid"), [Crazyhead](/wiki/Crazyhead "Crazyhead"), [the Hunters Club](/wiki/The_Hunters_Club "The Hunters Club") and [Scum Pups](/wiki/Scum_Pups "Scum Pups").[[88]](#cite_note-88)

### Mainstream–underground split: 1990s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_rock&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Mainstream–underground split: 1990s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Sonic_Youth_2009.05.30_001.jpg/250px-Sonic_Youth_2009.05.30_001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sonic_Youth_2009.05.30_001.jpg)

The success of grunge allowed [Sonic Youth](/wiki/Sonic_Youth "Sonic Youth") to break through into the mainstream.[[89]](#cite_note-Spin_breakthrough-89)

In the early 1990s, the Seattle [grunge](/wiki/Grunge "Grunge") scene, and its most visible acts, [Nirvana](/wiki/Nirvana_(band) "Nirvana (band)"), [Pearl Jam](/wiki/Pearl_Jam "Pearl Jam"), [Soundgarden](/wiki/Soundgarden "Soundgarden") and [Alice in Chains](/wiki/Alice_in_Chains "Alice in Chains"), broke into the mainstream.[[90]](#cite_note-90) The monumental success of these bands, particularly Nirvana, brought increased attention to the indie rock scene, which initiated a shift in which the indie rock descriptor became displaced by the term [alternative rock](/wiki/Alternative_rock "Alternative rock").[[89]](#cite_note-Spin_breakthrough-89) As a result, the term "alternative" lost its original counter-cultural meaning and began to refer to the new, commercially lighter form of music that was now achieving mainstream success. *[New York](/wiki/New_York_(magazine) "New York (magazine)")* magazine writer Carl Swanson argued that even the term "sellout" lost its meaning as grunge made it possible for a niche movement, no matter how radical, to be co-opted by the mainstream, cementing the formation of an individualist, fragmented culture.[[91]](#cite_note-Swanson-91)

In his book *Popular Music: The Key Concepts*, media academic Roy Shuker states that "Grunge represented the mainstreaming of the North American indie rock ethic and style of the 1980s", going on to explain that a band's status as independent became "As much a marketing device as [indie rock and alternative rock were an] identifiable 'sound'".[[92]](#cite_note-92) In the wake of this increased attention, indie rock experienced a split: accessible bands who catered to the now-popular alternative rock radio; and bands who continued to experiment, advancing in the underground.[[62]](#cite_note-AMCollegeRock-62) According to *[AllMusic](/wiki/AllMusic "AllMusic")*, it was during this split that "indie rock" solidified itself as a term for the style of music played by these underground artists, while the mainstream indie rock-influenced bands became termed alternative rock.[[11]](#cite_note-AllMusicIndie-11)

[Slowcore](/wiki/Slowcore "Slowcore") developed in the United States as a direct counterpoint to the rapid growth of grunge.[[93]](#cite_note-Rogers_p640-93) Although loosely defined, slowcore generally includes slow tempos, minimalist instrumentals and sad lyrics.[[94]](#cite_note-Far_Out_comeback-94) [Galaxie 500](/wiki/Galaxie_500 "Galaxie 500"), particularly their second album *[On Fire](/wiki/On_Fire_(Galaxie_500_album) "On Fire (Galaxie 500 album)")* (1989), were heavy influences on the genre,[[95]](#cite_note-DIS_Guide-95)[[96]](#cite_note-Earles_124_2-96) with *[Bandcamp Daily](/wiki/Bandcamp "Bandcamp")* writer Robert Rubsam, calling them the "fountainhead for all that would come".[[97]](#cite_note-Bandcamp_timeline-97) The first wave of bands in the genre included [Red House Painters](/wiki/Red_House_Painters "Red House Painters"), [Codeine](/wiki/Codeine_(band) "Codeine (band)"), [Bedhead](/wiki/Bedhead_(band) "Bedhead (band)"), [Ida](/wiki/Ida_(band) "Ida (band)") and [Low](/wiki/Low_(band) "Low (band)"). The genre originated from around the United States, with no geographic focus, and very little interaction between its artists.[[97]](#cite_note-Bandcamp_timeline-97)

A younger subset of grebo bands emerged around 1991, who were in turn labelled "fraggle" bands.[[98]](#cite_note-98) During this movement, the dominant sound was a style of indie rock that was heavily indebted to punk and Nirvana's album *[Bleach](/wiki/Bleach_(Nirvana_album) "Bleach (Nirvana album)")* album, while also occasionally making use of [drum machines](/wiki/Drum_machines "Drum machines").[[99]](#cite_note-Beaumont_2018-99) *[Gigwise](/wiki/Gigwise "Gigwise")* writer Steven Kline described the style as "filthy guitars, filthier hair and t-shirts only a mother would wash". Prominent fraggle acts included [Senseless Things](/wiki/Senseless_Things "Senseless Things"), [Mega City Four](/wiki/Mega_City_Four "Mega City Four") and [Carter the Unstoppable Sex Machine](/wiki/Carter_the_Unstoppable_Sex_Machine "Carter the Unstoppable Sex Machine").[[99]](#cite_note-Beaumont_2018-99)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Stevemalkmus%28by_Scott_Dudelson%29.jpg/250px-Stevemalkmus%28by_Scott_Dudelson%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stevemalkmus(by_Scott_Dudelson).jpg)

[Pavement](/wiki/Pavement_(band) "Pavement (band)") singer/guitarist [Stephen Malkmus](/wiki/Stephen_Malkmus "Stephen Malkmus")

*Spin* writer Charles Aaron described [Pavement](/wiki/Pavement_(band) "Pavement (band)") and [Guided by Voices](/wiki/Guided_by_Voices "Guided by Voices") as "the two bands that came to exemplify indie rock in this period, and still define the term in many people's minds". Both bands made use of a [lo-fi](/wiki/Lo-fi_music "Lo-fi music") production style which romanticised their D.I.Y. ethos.[[100]](#cite_note-100) Pavement's 1992 album *[Slanted and Enchanted](/wiki/Slanted_and_Enchanted "Slanted and Enchanted")*, was one of the defining albums of the [slacker rock](/wiki/Slacker_rock "Slacker rock") subgenre.[[101]](#cite_note-101) *Rolling Stone* called the album "the quintessential indie rock album", placing it on the magazine's list of [the 500 greatest albums of all time](/wiki/Rolling_Stone%27s_500_Greatest_Albums_of_All_Time "Rolling Stone's 500 Greatest Albums of All Time").[[102]](#cite_note-RS2012-102)

In the North Carolina [Research Triangle](/wiki/Research_Triangle "Research Triangle"), an indie rock scene was being spearheaded by groups enfranched [Merge Records](/wiki/Merge_Records "Merge Records") like [Superchunk](/wiki/Superchunk "Superchunk"), [Archers of Loaf](/wiki/Archers_of_Loaf "Archers of Loaf") and [Polvo](/wiki/Polvo "Polvo").
describing a growing scene of indie-rock bands who were influenced by hardcore punk and post-punk.[[103]](#cite_note-103) At the time, publications such as *[Entertainment Weekly](/wiki/Entertainment_Weekly "Entertainment Weekly")* took to calling the college town of [Chapel Hill](/wiki/Chapel_Hill,_North_Carolina "Chapel Hill, North Carolina") the "next Seattle".[[104]](#cite_note-104)[[105]](#cite_note-105) Superchunk's single "[Slack Motherfucker](/wiki/Slack_Motherfucker "Slack Motherfucker")" has also been credited by *Columbia* magazine with popularizing the "[slacker](/wiki/Slacker "Slacker")" stereotype, and as a defining anthem of 90s indie rock.[[106]](#cite_note-106)

With the rise of [Britpop](/wiki/Britpop "Britpop"), many of Britain's earlier indie rock bands fell into obscurity.[[107]](#cite_note-107) Fronted by [Blur](/wiki/Blur_(band) "Blur (band)"), [Oasis](/wiki/Oasis_(band) "Oasis (band)"), [Pulp](/wiki/Pulp_(band) "Pulp (band)") and [Suede](/wiki/Suede_(band) "Suede (band)"),[[108]](#cite_note-108) the bands in the movement were advertised as being underground artists, as a means to compete commercially with the United States' grunge scene.[[109]](#cite_note-109) While Britpop was stylistically indebted to indie rock and began as an offshoot of it, Britpop bands abandoned the genre's earlier anti-establishment politics and instead brought it into the mainstream, with bands like Blur and Pulp even signing to major labels. In her essay *Labouring the Point? The Politics of Britain in "New Britain"*, politician and academic [Rupa Huq](/wiki/Rupa_Huq "Rupa Huq") states that Britpop "began as an offshoot of the independent British music scene but arguably ended up killing it, as a convergence took place between indie and mainstream, removing the distinctive 'protest' element of British-based independent music"[[15]](#cite_note-Huq_2016-15) Music journalist [John Harris](/wiki/John_Harris_(critic) "John Harris (critic)") has suggested that Britpop began when Blur's fourth single "[Popscene](/wiki/Popscene "Popscene")" and Suede's debut "[The Drowners](/wiki/The_Drowners "The Drowners")" were released around the same time in the spring of 1992. He stated, "[I]f Britpop started anywhere, it was the deluge of acclaim that greeted Suede's first records: all of them audacious, successful and very, very British."[[110]](#cite_note-110) Suede were the first of the new crop of guitar-orientated bands to be embraced by the UK music media as Britain's answer to Seattle's grunge sound. Their debut album *[Suede](/wiki/Suede_(album) "Suede (album)")*, released on 29 March 1993, became the fastest-selling debut album in British music history at the time.[[111]](#cite_note-British_alt-rock-111)

#### Diversification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_rock&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Diversification")]

[Sunny Day Real Estate](/wiki/Sunny_Day_Real_Estate "Sunny Day Real Estate")'s debut album, *[Diary](/wiki/Diary_(Sunny_Day_Real_Estate_album) "Diary (Sunny Day Real Estate album)")* (1994), began a new wave of the [emo](/wiki/Emo "Emo") genre, by incorporating elements of it into their indie rock sound.[[112]](#cite_note-Cateforis_2013-112) Sunny Day Real Estate and other second wave emo bands, including [Piebald](/wiki/Piebald_(band) "Piebald (band)"), [the Promise Ring](/wiki/The_Promise_Ring "The Promise Ring") and [Cap'n Jazz](/wiki/Cap%27n_Jazz "Cap'n Jazz") distanced emo from its hardcore roots and allowed the genre to develop a much more realised scene than its first wave.[[113]](#cite_note-Connick-113) This style of emo broke into mainstream culture in the early 2000s, with the platinum-selling success of [Jimmy Eat World](/wiki/Jimmy_Eat_World "Jimmy Eat World")'s *[Bleed American](/wiki/Bleed_American "Bleed American")* (2001) and [Dashboard Confessional](/wiki/Dashboard_Confessional "Dashboard Confessional")'s *[The Places You Have Come to Fear the Most](/wiki/The_Places_You_Have_Come_to_Fear_the_Most "The Places You Have Come to Fear the Most")* (2001).[[114]](#cite_note-DeRogatis2003p373-4-114) One particularly notable scene during this wave was the [Midwest emo](/wiki/Midwest_emo "Midwest emo") bands of the latter half of the decade, who incorporated the jangly guitar tones of earlier indie rock and elements of [math rock](/wiki/Math_rock "Math rock") to create the distinctive style of groups like [American Football](/wiki/American_Football_(band) "American Football (band)").[[113]](#cite_note-Connick-113) The popularity of emo, also allowed a number of "not-quite-indie-not-quite-emo" bands like [Death Cab For Cutie](/wiki/Death_Cab_For_Cutie "Death Cab For Cutie"), [Modest Mouse](/wiki/Modest_Mouse "Modest Mouse") and [Karate](/wiki/Karate_(band) "Karate (band)") to gain significant attention.[[115]](#cite_note-115)

The loosely defined [Elephant 6](/wiki/Elephant_6 "Elephant 6") collective – which included [the Apples in Stereo](/wiki/The_Apples_in_Stereo "The Apples in Stereo"), [Beulah](/wiki/Beulah_(band) "Beulah (band)"), [Circulatory System](/wiki/Circulatory_System_(band) "Circulatory System (band)"), [Elf Power](/wiki/Elf_Power "Elf Power"), [the Minders](/wiki/The_Minders "The Minders"), [Neutral Milk Hotel](/wiki/Neutral_Milk_Hotel "Neutral Milk Hotel"), [of Montreal](/wiki/Of_Montreal "Of Montreal") and [the Olivia Tremor Control](/wiki/The_Olivia_Tremor_Control "The Olivia Tremor Control") – merged indie rock with [psychedelic pop](/wiki/Psychedelic_pop "Psychedelic pop"). *[Gimme Indie Rock](/wiki/Gimme_Indie_Rock:_500_Essential_American_Underground_Rock_Albums_1981%E2%80%931996 "Gimme Indie Rock: 500 Essential American Underground Rock Albums 1981–1996")* author Andrew Earles stated that the collective, namely Neutral Milk Hotel on *[On Avery Island](/wiki/On_Avery_Island "On Avery Island")* (1996), "helped keep the genre artistically relevant while other bands defected and other underground styles rose to prominence".[[116]](#cite_note-116)[[117]](#cite_note-117)

Indie electronic or indietronica[[118]](#cite_note-118) covers rock-based artists who share an affinity for electronic music, using samplers, synthesizers, drum machines, and computer programs.[[119]](#cite_note-AMGO-119) Less a style and more a categorization, it describes an early 1990s trend of acts who followed in the traditions of early electronic music (composers of the [BBC Radiophonic Workshop](/wiki/BBC_Radiophonic_Workshop "BBC Radiophonic Workshop")), [krautrock](/wiki/Krautrock "Krautrock") and [synth-pop](/wiki/Synth-pop "Synth-pop").[[119]](#cite_note-AMGO-119) Progenitors of the genre were English bands [Disco Inferno](/wiki/Disco_Inferno_(band) "Disco Inferno (band)"), [Stereolab](/wiki/Stereolab "Stereolab"), and [Space](/wiki/Space_(UK_band) "Space (UK band)").[[119]](#cite_note-AMGO-119) Most musicians in the genre can be found on independent labels like [Warp](/wiki/Warp_(record_label) "Warp (record label)"), [Morr Music](/wiki/Morr_Music "Morr Music"), [Sub Pop](/wiki/Sub_Pop "Sub Pop") or [Ghostly International](/wiki/Ghostly_International "Ghostly International").[[119]](#cite_note-AMGO-119)

[Space rock](/wiki/Space_rock "Space rock") took the [psychedelic rock](/wiki/Psychedelic_rock "Psychedelic rock"), [ambient music](/wiki/Ambient_music "Ambient music") influence of [Pink Floyd](/wiki/Pink_Floyd "Pink Floyd") and [Hawkwind](/wiki/Hawkwind "Hawkwind") and incorporated them into an indie rock context. The style began with [Spacemen 3](/wiki/Spacemen_3 "Spacemen 3") in the 1980s, with later groups including [Spiritualized](/wiki/Spiritualized "Spiritualized"), [Flying Saucer Attack](/wiki/Flying_Saucer_Attack "Flying Saucer Attack"), [Godspeed You! Black Emperor](/wiki/Godspeed_You!_Black_Emperor "Godspeed You! Black Emperor") and [Quickspace](/wiki/Quickspace "Quickspace").[[120]](#cite_note-120)

As Britpop waned towards the end of the decade, [post-Britpop](/wiki/Post-Britpop "Post-Britpop") took hold within the UK's indie rock scene.[[121]](#cite_note-121)
From about 1997, as dissatisfaction grew with the concept of [Cool Britannia](/wiki/Cool_Britannia "Cool Britannia") and Britpop as a movement began to dissolve, emerging bands began to avoid the Britpop label while still producing music derived from it.[[122]](#cite_note-Harris2004-122)[[123]](#cite_note-Borhwick&Moy2004-123) After the decline of Britpop they began to gain more critical and popular attention.[[122]](#cite_note-Harris2004-122) [The Verve](/wiki/The_Verve "The Verve")'s album *[Urban Hymns](/wiki/Urban_Hymns "Urban Hymns")* (1997) was a worldwide hit and their commercial peak before they broke up in 1999, while [Radiohead](/wiki/Radiohead "Radiohead") – although having achieved moderate recognition with *[The Bends](/wiki/The_Bends_(album) "The Bends (album)")* in 1995 – achieved near-universal critical acclaim with their experimental third album *[OK Computer](/wiki/OK_Computer "OK Computer")* (1997), and its follow-ups *[Kid A](/wiki/Kid_A "Kid A")* (2000) and *[Amnesiac](/wiki/Amnesiac_(album) "Amnesiac (album)")* (2001).[[124]](#cite_note-Bogdanov2002Radiohead&Verve-124) [Stereophonics](/wiki/Stereophonics "Stereophonics"), used elements of a post-grunge and hardcore on their breakthrough albums *[Word Gets Around](/wiki/Word_Gets_Around "Word Gets Around")* (1997) and *[Performance and Cocktails](/wiki/Performance_and_Cocktails "Performance and Cocktails")* (1999), before moving into more melodic territory with *[Just Enough Education to Perform](/wiki/Just_Enough_Education_to_Perform "Just Enough Education to Perform")* (2001) and subsequent albums.[[125]](#cite_note-Bogdanov2002Stereophonics-125)[[126]](#cite_note-AllMusicStereophonics-126) [Feeder](/wiki/Feeder_(band) "Feeder (band)"), who were initially more influenced by American post-grunge, producing a hard rock sound that led to their breakthrough single "[Buck Rogers](/wiki/Buck_Rogers_(song) "Buck Rogers (song)")" and the album *[Echo Park](/wiki/Echo_Park_(album) "Echo Park (album)")* (2001).[[127]](#cite_note-127) After the death of their drummer [Jon Lee](/wiki/Jon_Lee_(drummer) "Jon Lee (drummer)"), they moved to a more reflective and introspective mode on *[Comfort in Sound](/wiki/Comfort_in_Sound "Comfort in Sound")* (2002), their most commercially successful album to that point, which spawned a series of hit singles.[[128]](#cite_note-128) The most commercially successful band in the millennium were [Coldplay](/wiki/Coldplay "Coldplay"), whose first two albums *[Parachutes](/wiki/Parachutes_(Coldplay_album) "Parachutes (Coldplay album)")* (2000) and *[A Rush of Blood to the Head](/wiki/A_Rush_of_Blood_to_the_Head "A Rush of Blood to the Head")* (2002) went [multi-platinum](/wiki/Music_recording_sales_certification "Music recording sales certification"), establishing them as one of the most popular acts in the world by the time of their third album *[X&Y](/wiki/X%26Y "X&Y")* (2005).[[129]](#cite_note-AllMusicColdplay-129)[[130]](#cite_note-130) Snow Patrol's "[Chasing Cars](/wiki/Chasing_Cars "Chasing Cars")" (from their 2006 album *[Eyes Open](/wiki/Eyes_Open_(Snow_Patrol_album) "Eyes Open (Snow Patrol album)")*) is the most widely played song of the 21st century on UK radio.[[131]](#cite_note-131)

### Mainstream success: 2000s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_rock&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Mainstream success: 2000s")]

#### Post-punk and garage rock revival

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_rock&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Post-punk and garage rock revival")]

Main articles: [Post-punk revival](/wiki/Post-punk_revival "Post-punk revival") and [Garage rock revival](/wiki/Garage_rock_revival "Garage rock revival")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/The_Strokes_by_Roger_Woolman.jpg/250px-The_Strokes_by_Roger_Woolman.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Strokes_by_Roger_Woolman.jpg)

[The Strokes](/wiki/The_Strokes "The Strokes") are one of the most influential bands to indie rock in the 2000s.

The mainstream attention which indie rock garnered in the 2000s began with [the Strokes](/wiki/The_Strokes "The Strokes") and their 2001 debut album *[Is This It](/wiki/Is_This_It "Is This It")*. Playing a style indebted to '60s-70s bands like [the Velvet Underground](/wiki/The_Velvet_Underground "The Velvet Underground") and [the Ramones](/wiki/The_Ramones "The Ramones"), the band's intention musically was to sound like "a band from the past that took a time trip into the future to make their record."[[132]](#cite_note-Indie88-132) The album peaked at number thirty-three in the United States, staying in the charts for two additional years and debuted at number two on the [UK albums chart](/wiki/UK_albums_chart "UK albums chart").[[132]](#cite_note-Indie88-132)[[133]](#cite_note-133) When the Strokes made their commercial debut, the public perception of "rock music" was based in [post-grunge](/wiki/Post-grunge "Post-grunge"), [nu metal](/wiki/Nu_metal "Nu metal") and [rap rock](/wiki/Rap_rock "Rap rock"), putting their throwback style of [garage rock](/wiki/Garage_rock "Garage rock") as a stark contrast to the mainstream. The band's immediate influence allowed fellow classic rock influenced New York bands like the [Yeah Yeah Yeahs](/wiki/Yeah_Yeah_Yeahs "Yeah Yeah Yeahs"), [Interpol](/wiki/Interpol_(band) "Interpol (band)") and [TV on the Radio](/wiki/TV_on_the_Radio "TV on the Radio") to gain mainstream attention.[[134]](#cite_note-Golsen_2022-134) The Strokes were accompanied in this commercial breakthrough by [the White Stripes](/wiki/The_White_Stripes "The White Stripes"), [the Vines](/wiki/The_Vines_(band) "The Vines (band)"), and [the Hives](/wiki/The_Hives "The Hives"). These groups were christened by parts of the media as the "The" bands, and dubbed "the saviours of rock 'n' roll",[[135]](#cite_note-Smith2009-135) prompting *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")* magazine to declare on its September 2002 cover, "Rock is Back!"[[136]](#cite_note-Youngs2002-136)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/The_Libertines_%2840466665072%29.jpg/250px-The_Libertines_%2840466665072%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Libertines_(40466665072).jpg)

[The Libertines](/wiki/The_Libertines "The Libertines") were described by [AllMusic](/wiki/AllMusic "AllMusic") as "one of the U.K.'s most influential 21st century acts".

The success of the Strokes revitalised the then-dying underground post-Britpop scene in the United Kingdom with groups who took the band's influence and experimented with their sound. This first wave of UK acts included [Franz Ferdinand](/wiki/Franz_Ferdinand_(band) "Franz Ferdinand (band)"), [Kasabian](/wiki/Kasabian "Kasabian"), [Maxïmo Park](/wiki/Max%C3%AFmo_Park "Maxïmo Park"), [the Cribs](/wiki/The_Cribs "The Cribs"), [Bloc Party](/wiki/Bloc_Party "Bloc Party"), [Kaiser Chiefs](/wiki/Kaiser_Chiefs "Kaiser Chiefs") and [the Others](/wiki/The_Others_(band) "The Others (band)").[[137]](#cite_note-:0-137) However, [the Libertines](/wiki/The_Libertines "The Libertines"), who formed in 1997, stood as the UK's counterpoint to the Strokes, being described by [AllMusic](/wiki/AllMusic "AllMusic") as "one of the U.K.'s most influential 21st century acts"[[138]](#cite_note-Phares,_AllMusic-138) and *[The Independent](/wiki/The_Independent "The Independent")* stating that "the Libertines wanted to be an important band, but they could not have predicted the impact they would have".[[139]](#cite_note-independent0703-139) Influenced by [the Clash](/wiki/The_Clash "The Clash"), [the Kinks](/wiki/The_Kinks "The Kinks"), [the Smiths](/wiki/The_Smiths "The Smiths") and [the Jam](/wiki/The_Jam "The Jam"),[[138]](#cite_note-Phares,_AllMusic-138) the band's style of tinny, high register, sometimes acoustic, guitar parts topped by lyrics of British [parochial](/wiki/Parochialism "Parochialism") pleasures in the vocalists' authentic English accents became widely imitated.[[139]](#cite_note-independent0703-139) [The Fratellis](/wiki/The_Fratellis "The Fratellis"), [the Kooks](/wiki/The_Kooks "The Kooks"), and [the View](/wiki/The_View_(band) "The View (band)") were three such acts to gain significant commercial success, although the most prominent post-Libertines band was Sheffield's [Arctic Monkeys](/wiki/Arctic_Monkeys "Arctic Monkeys").[[139]](#cite_note-independent0703-139) One of the earliest groups to owe their initial commercial success to the use of [Internet social networking](/wiki/Internet_social_network "Internet social network"),[[140]](#cite_note-140) the Arctic Monkeys had two No. 1 singles, and their album *[Whatever People Say I Am, That's What I'm Not](/wiki/Whatever_People_Say_I_Am,_That%27s_What_I%27m_Not "Whatever People Say I Am, That's What I'm Not")* (2006) became the fastest-selling debut album in British chart history.[[141]](#cite_note-141)

In this success, legacy indie bands soon entered the mainstream,[[142]](#cite_note-Spitz2010-142) including Modest Mouse (whose 2004 album *[Good News for People Who Love Bad News](/wiki/Good_News_for_People_Who_Love_Bad_News "Good News for People Who Love Bad News")* reached the US top 40 and was nominated for a [Grammy](/wiki/Grammy "Grammy") Award), [Bright Eyes](/wiki/Bright_Eyes_(band) "Bright Eyes (band)") (who in 2004 had two singles at the top of the Billboard magazine [Hot 100 Single Sales](/wiki/Hot_100_Single_Sales "Hot 100 Single Sales"))[[143]](#cite_note-143) and Death Cab for Cutie (whose 2005 album *[Plans](/wiki/Plans_(album) "Plans (album)")* debuted at number four in the US, remaining on the Billboard charts for nearly one year and achieving platinum status and a Grammy nomination).[[144]](#cite_note-144) This new commercial breakthrough and the widespread use of the term indie to other forms of popular culture, led a number of commentators to suggest that indie rock had ceased to be a meaningful term.[[145]](#cite_note-145)[[146]](#cite_note-146)

Additionally, a second wave of bands emerged in the United States that managed to gain international recognition as a result of the movement included [the Black Keys](/wiki/The_Black_Keys "The Black Keys"), [Kings of Leon](/wiki/Kings_of_Leon "Kings of Leon"), [the Shins](/wiki/The_Shins "The Shins"), [the Bravery](/wiki/The_Bravery "The Bravery"), [Spoon](/wiki/Spoon_(band) "Spoon (band)"), [the Hold Steady](/wiki/The_Hold_Steady "The Hold Steady"), and [the National](/wiki/The_National_(band) "The National (band)").[[114]](#cite_note-DeRogatis2003p373-4-114) The most commercially successful band of this wave was Las Vegas' [the Killers](/wiki/The_Killers "The Killers"). Formed in 2001, after hearing *Is This It*, the band scrapped the majority of their prior material to rewrite it under the Strokes' influence.[[147]](#cite_note-147) The band's debut single "[Mr. Brightside](/wiki/Mr._Brightside "Mr. Brightside")" spent 260 non-consecutive weeks, or five years, on the [UK Singles Chart](/wiki/UK_Singles_Chart "UK Singles Chart") as of April 2021, the most out of any song,[[148]](#cite_note-copsey-148)[[149]](#cite_note-moore-149) and As of 2017[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Indie_rock&action=edit), it had charted on the UK Singles Chart in 11 of the last 13 years,[[150]](#cite_note-150) including a 35-week run peaking at number 49 in 2016–2017.[[151]](#cite_note-MetChart-151) Furthermore, it was the UK's most streamed pre-2010 song, until it was surpassed in late 2018,[[152]](#cite_note-152) and continued to be purchased for download hundreds of times a week by 2017.[[153]](#cite_note-200weeks-153) In March 2018, the song reached the milestone of staying in the Top 100 of the UK Singles Chart for 200 weeks.[[153]](#cite_note-200weeks-153)

#### Landfill indie

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_rock&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Landfill indie")]

Main article: [Landfill indie](/wiki/Landfill_indie "Landfill indie")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Alex_Turner_and_Nick_O%27Malley_Roskilde_2014.jpg/250px-Alex_Turner_and_Nick_O%27Malley_Roskilde_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alex_Turner_and_Nick_O%27Malley_Roskilde_2014.jpg)

[Arctic Monkeys](/wiki/Arctic_Monkeys "Arctic Monkeys") are one of the most commercially successful bands to emerge out of the [landfill indie](/wiki/Landfill_indie "Landfill indie") movement.

The impact of the Strokes, the Libertines and Bloc Party led to significant major label interest in indie rock artists, which was then exacerbated by the success of the Arctic Monkeys. In the years following *Whatever People Say I Am, That's What I'm Not* there was a proliferation of bands such as [the Rifles](/wiki/The_Rifles_(band) "The Rifles (band)"), [the Pigeon Detectives](/wiki/The_Pigeon_Detectives "The Pigeon Detectives") and [Milburn](/wiki/Milburn_(band) "Milburn (band)"), who created a more formulaic derivative of the earlier acts.[[154]](#cite_note-vice.com-154)[[137]](#cite_note-:0-137) By the end of the decade, critics had taken to referring to this wave of acts as "[landfill indie](/wiki/Landfill_indie "Landfill indie")",[[155]](#cite_note-155)[[156]](#cite_note-Beaumont_2020-156)[[157]](#cite_note-T._Walker-157) a description coined by [Andrew Harrison](/wiki/Andrew_Harrison_(journalist) "Andrew Harrison (journalist)") of *[the Word](/wiki/The_Word_(UK_magazine) "The Word (UK magazine)")* magazine.[[158]](#cite_note-158) A 2020 *[Vice](/wiki/Vice_Media "Vice Media")* article cited [Johnny Borrell](/wiki/Johnny_Borrell "Johnny Borrell"), vocalist of [Razorlight](/wiki/Razorlight "Razorlight"), as the "one man who defined, embodied and lived Landfill Indie" due to his forming of a "spectacularly middle-of-the-road" band despite his close proximity to the Libertines' "desperate kinetic energy, mythologised love-hate dynamic and vision of a dilapidated Britain animated by romance and narcotics".[[154]](#cite_note-vice.com-154) In a 2009 article for *[the Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")*, journalist [Peter Robinson](/wiki/Peter_Robinson_(journalist) "Peter Robinson (journalist)") cited the landfill indie movement as dead, blaming [the Wombats](/wiki/The_Wombats "The Wombats"), [Scouting For Girls](/wiki/Scouting_For_Girls "Scouting For Girls"), and [Joe Lean & the Jing Jang Jong](/wiki/Joe_Lean_%26_the_Jing_Jang_Jong "Joe Lean & the Jing Jang Jong") by stating "If landfill indie had been a game of *[Buckaroo](/wiki/Buckaroo! "Buckaroo!")*, those three sent the whole donkey's arse of radio-friendly mainstream guitar band monotony flying high into the air, legs flailing."[[159]](#cite_note-159)

### Continued success: 2010s–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indie_rock&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Continued success: 2010s–present")]

There continued to be commercial successes in the 2010s [Arcade Fire](/wiki/Arcade_Fire "Arcade Fire")'s *[The Suburbs](/wiki/The_Suburbs_(Arcade_Fire_album) "The Suburbs (Arcade Fire album)")* (2010), the Black Keys's *[Turn Blue](/wiki/Turn_Blue_(album) "Turn Blue (album)")* (2014), Kings of Leon's *[Walls](/wiki/Walls_(Kings_of_Leon_album) "Walls (Kings of Leon album)")* (2016), the Killers's *[Wonderful Wonderful](/wiki/Wonderful_Wonderful_(The_Killers_album) "Wonderful Wonderful (The Killers album)")* (2017), which reached number one on the Billboard charts in the United States and the official chart in the United Kingdom, with Arcade Fire's album winning a Grammy for Album of The Year in 2011.[[160]](#cite_note-160) Other indie rock acts like [Florence and the Machine](/wiki/Florence_and_the_Machine "Florence and the Machine"), [the Decemberists](/wiki/The_Decemberists "The Decemberists") and [LCD Soundsystem](/wiki/LCD_Soundsystem "LCD Soundsystem") gained number one singles in the United States during the decade, with [Vampire Weekend](/wiki/Vampire_Weekend "Vampire Weekend"), Florence and the Machine, Arctic Monkeys, [Bon Iver](/wiki/Bon_Iver "Bon Iver"), the Killers and [the Postal Service](/wiki/The_Postal_Service "The Postal Service") gaining platinum selling records.[[161]](#cite_note-Clark_2019-161) Vampire Weekend's third studio album *[Modern Vampires of the City](/wiki/Modern_Vampires_of_the_City "Modern Vampires of the City")* (2013) received the [Grammy Award for Best Alternative Music Album](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Alternative_Music_Album "Grammy Award for Best Alternative Music Album") in 2014, with [Consequence](/wiki/Consequence_(publication) "Consequence (publication)") writer Tyler Clark stating that in 2019 it was still "an indie rock standard bearer in the wider world of music".[[161]](#cite_note-Clark_2019-161) Arctic Monkeys' fifth album *[AM](/wiki/AM_(Arctic_Monkeys_album) "AM (Arctic Monkeys album)")* (2013) was one of the biggest indie rock albums of the decade, charting at number one on the [UK Albums Chart](/wiki/UK_Albums_Chart "UK Albums Chart"), having sold 157,329 copies, thus becoming the second fastest-selling album of the year.[[162]](#cite_note-162) With the debut of *AM* on the chart, Arctic Monkeys also broke a record, becoming the first independent-label band to debut at number one in the UK with their first five albums.[[163]](#cite_note-OCC-163) As of June 2019, *AM* has spent 300 weeks in the top 100 of the [UK Albums Chart](/wiki/UK_Albums_Chart "UK Albums Chart").[[164]](#cite_note-Sound_Mouth-164) The album also peaked at number one in Australia, Belgium (Flanders), Croatia, Slovenia, Denmark, Ireland, the Netherlands, New Zealand, and Portugal, and reached top ten positions in several other countries. In the United States, the album sold 42,000 copies in its first week, and debuted at number six on the *Billboard* 200 chart, becoming the band's highest-charting album in the United States.[[165]](#cite_note-US_sales-165) In August 2017, *AM* was certified [platinum](/wiki/RIAA_certification "RIAA certification") by the [RIAA](/wiki/RIAA "RIAA") for combined sales and [album-equivalent units](/wiki/Album-equivalent_unit "Album-equivalent unit") over of a million units in the United States.[[166]](#cite_note-US2-166) As of the 14th of April 2023 every track from the album was certified silver or higher by the BPI with "Mad Sounds" being the last to be certified.[[167]](#cite_note-BPI-167)

When [the 1975](/wiki/The_1975 "The 1975")'s merger of indie rock and mainstream pop began gaining commercial attraction in the early 2010s, it was controversial; they received the award for "Worst Band" at the 2014 *[NME Awards](/wiki/NME_Awards "NME Awards")*, but by 2017 received "Best Live Band" at the same award show.[[168]](#cite_note-168) *[Alternative Press](/wiki/Alternative_Press_(magazine) "Alternative Press (magazine)")* writer Yasmine Summan stated that "If you could summarize 2013 and 2014 in one album for indie and alternative fans, it would be the 1975's [self-titled release](/wiki/The_1975_(album) "The 1975 (album)")".[[169]](#cite_note-169) In an article for *[the Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* accrediting the 1975 as the band to "usher indie into the mainstream", writer Mark Beaumont compared vocalist Matty Healy's influence on the genre to that of Libertines vocalist [Pete Doherty](/wiki/Pete_Doherty "Pete Doherty"),[[170]](#cite_note-170) and *[Pitchfork](/wiki/Pitchfork_(website) "Pitchfork (website)")* listed them as one of the most influential artists in music since 1995.[[171]](#cite_note-171) In the 1975's wake, a number of other [indie pop](/wiki/Indie_pop "Indie pop") artists gained popularity. Some critics termed this phenomenon "Healywave", which notably included: [Pale Waves](/wiki/Pale_Waves "Pale Waves"), [the Aces](/wiki/The_Aces_(indie_pop_band) "The Aces (indie pop band)"), [Joan](/wiki/Joan_(band) "Joan (band)"), [Fickle Friends](/wiki/Fickle_Friends "Fickle Friends") and [No Rome](/wiki/No_Rome "No Rome").[[172]](#cite_note-172) Of this group, Pale Waves were particularly commercially prominent, with their debut album *[My Mind Makes Noises](/wiki/My_Mind_Makes_Noises "My Mind Makes Noises")* peaking at number eight on the UK albums chart, *[Who Am I?](/wiki/Who_Am_I%3F_(Pale_Waves_album) "Who Am I? (Pale Waves album)")* (2021) at number three and *[Unwanted](/wiki/Unwanted_(album) "Unwanted (album)")* (2022) at number four.[[173]](#cite_note-173) At around the same time [Wolf Alice](/wiki/Wolf_Alice "Wolf Alice") became a prominent force in the scene, with their second album *[Visions of a Life](/wiki/Visions_of_a_Life "Visions of a Life")* (2017) winning the Mercury Prize in 2018 and third album *[Blue Weekend](/wiki/Blue_Weekend "Blue Weekend")* (2021) being nominated.[[174]](#cite_note-174) Writer Martin Young stated in a 2021 article for [Dork](/wiki/Dork_(magazine) "Dork (magazine)") that "It's impossible to truly state just how important Wolf Alice are. They are the catalyst for almost all the amazing bands you've read about in *Dork* over the last 5 years."[[175]](#cite_note-175)

"But the two bands that came to exemplify indie rock in this period, and still define the term in many people's minds, were Pavement and Guided by Voices. A furtive collective from Northern California led by singer/guitarists "SM" (Stephen Malkmus) and "Spiral Stairs" (Scott Kannberg), Pavement created lo-fi bedroom dramas that romanticized the misty bewilderment of college kids on the cusp of entering the adult straight world. Their free-associating lyrics over melodies that seemed embedded in the staticky fuzz of a transistor radio were a cryptic denial of alt rock's ambition. Malkmus had a gift (like Kurt Cobain) for inside-joke aphorisms that felt like generational broadsides. On the 1992 album Slanted and Enchanted, he mused with a stricken hauteur: "I've got a lot of things I want to sell / But not here, babe," and "All the things we had before / You sold us out and took it all." Guided by Voices, a revolving-door group led by thirtysomething Dayton, Ohio schoolteacher Robert Pollard, had been churning out blearily melodic, cruddy-sounding recordings for years, but their literate, elliptical bursts of British Invasion-tinged rock never caught much attention. However, with the release of 1994's breathtakingly composed Bee Thousand, Pollard was suddenly a mini cause célèbre, a symbol of the uncompromising, self-reliant indie artist who eventually gets the recognition he deserves."
